hitBox.label: asymptotic series
hitBox.sequence: the sum from k equals zero to infinity of epsilon to the k times f sub k

hitBox.label: remainder definition
hitBox.sequence: f of epsilon minus the sum from k equals zero to m of epsilon to the k times f sub k is little o of epsilon to the m for all m

mappings.value: f of epsilon is asymptotic to the series
the sum from k equals zero to infinity of epsilon to the k times f sub k
if and only if, after truncating the series at any finite order
little o of epsilon to the m
. Here epsilon is the small parameter approaching zero, f sub k are the asymptotic coefficients, and m is the truncation order.
Equation 16 defines what it means for a full asymptotic series to represent
. The point is not exact convergence of the infinite sum. The point is that each finite truncation captures the function up to an error smaller than the last retained power.

Conceptually, Eq. 16 upgrades Eq. 15 from comparing two single expressions to comparing a function with an entire asymptotic expansion. It explains why near-threshold derivations keep only the leading powers that control the local behavior.
